narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance, family background, and test scores to predict the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your third-year school decile of 1, undergraduate GPA of 3.40, lower-middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 28, first-year decile of 3, and full-time enrollment plans—and predicted a 47% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your low ranking in third-year grades compared to peers, where you placed in the bottom decile of your school (far below the typical fifth-decile average), signaling to the model a potential struggle with advanced legal coursework. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which is stronger than the average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction toward success by demonstrating solid prior academic ability. Your lower-middle family income also worked in your favor, as it nudged the model toward viewing you as more likely to pass, perhaps reflecting resilience often seen in similar backgrounds. While your LSAT score of 28 is notably below the average of 36.7, it wasn't the top driver here, though it contributed to the overall concern. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your third-year performance stands out as the biggest deviation, while your undergrad GPA aligns favorably with successful applicants. Full-time status, common among 93% of students, didn't sway the decision much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male pursuing law—many from similar backgrounds overcome early hurdles—but the model weighs these patterns heavily to minimize bar failure risks for the school. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and reapplying with updated transcripts that highlight improvement. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or bridge courses to build a stronger case, turning this setback into a stepping stone for success.
